+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 78.0
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The saxophone is playing something blue and bent, and I am walking, and my feet hurt because yesterday I was wearing sneakers but today I’m wearing these cowboy boots that were supposed to make me look older, more *city*, less like some hick kid who thinks running away means freedom instead of just a different kind of cage. The notes follow me down Royal Street, sticky and slow like the heat that’s already pressing against my binder, making it hard to breathe right. *Two states*, I keep thinking. *Two whole states between me and that house.* But two states doesn’t feel like enough when you’re sixteen and your wallet contains exactly four dollars, a crushed pack of gum, and an ID that has the wrong name on it. The wrong picture. The wrong *everything*. I stop at a corner where a guy is selling pralines from a cart that smells like burnt sugar and regret. My stomach makes this noise that sounds like a dying cat. I haven’t eaten since the truck driver—that greasy angel with the meth mouth who’d picked me up outside Memphis—shared his gas station taquitos at 3 AM before dropping me here with a grunt and a “good luck, kid, you’ll need it.” “Help you?” The praline guy doesn’t look up. He’s seen kids like me before. I can tell by the way his shoulders go stiff. “Just looking,” I say, pitching my voice lower than it wants to go. It cracks anyway. Seventeen days on T—self-sourced, internet-researched, probably questionable—and my throat still betrays me every third sentence. I move on. The saxophone is closer now, wailing from a doorway where a guy with dreads and no shoes is playing for tourists who throw coins like he’s a fountain. I want to sit down. I want to cry. I want to burn my birth certificate and also call my mom and tell her I’m sorry, so sorry, please just use the right pronouns and I’ll come home. Instead, I duck into an alley that smells like piss and jasmine, and that’s where I see them. They’re sitting on a milk crate, sketching in a notebook with charcoal-stained fingers. Black hair shaved on one side, hanging long and tangled on the other. Tank top showing arms that are all sharp angles and stick-and-poke tattoos. Older than me—eighteen, nineteen maybe. They look up, and their eyes are this weird amber color, like whiskey in sunlight. “You’re vibrating,” they say. “What?” “You’re literally shaking. Either you’re coming down off something, or you’re about to pass out.” They cap the charcoal. “Sit before you fall.” It’s not a kindness. It’s an observation. But my knees buckle anyway, and suddenly I’m on the concrete, back against brick that’s hot even in the shade. The saxophone filters in from the street, muffled now, a heartbeat. “I’m Jules,” they say. Not offering a hand. Just stating a fact. “Taylor.” They nod, slow. “You’re new.” “Just visiting.” “Bullshit. You’ve got that look. The ‘I just jumped off the Greyhound and realized the world is bigger than the map’ look.” Jules opens a beaten backpack and pulls out a warm Sprite. Tosses it to me. “Drink. You’re gray.” I catch it. My hands are shaking, just like they said. The soda burns going down, sugar and acid, and I nearly gag but keep swallowing because it’s *something*. “Thanks.” “Don’t thank me yet.” Jules goes back to sketching. It’s the alley they’re drawing—the bricks, the graffiti, the way the light hits the trash. It’s good. “Where’d you blow in from?” I hesitate. The counsellor’s voice is in my head, nasal and patronizing: *concerning behavioural patterns*. My mother’s voice, broken: *You’re just confused. You’re my daughter.* “Tennessee.” “Shit state,” Jules says mildly. “I’m from Baton Rouge, which is only slightly less shit. Ran last year.” The word *ran* hangs between us like a spiderweb. I pick at the tab on the soda can. “You live here? On the street?” “Sometimes. Sometimes I couch-surf. Sometimes I make enough drawing tourists to get a motel room by the highway.” They glance at me, sharp. “You got a plan, Tennessee?” “Not really.” “Family?” “Not anymore.” The words come out harder than I mean them to. Sharp edges. Jules stops drawing. Studies me with that amber gaze that feels like it can see the binder under my shirt, the panic in my chest, the way I’m trying so hard to be a boy in a world that keeps calling me girl. “You’re trans,” they say. Not a question. My throat closes. “How did you—” “Please. I’ve been out here fifteen months. I can spot family from a mile away. The way you hold yourself. The binder.” They tap their own chest. “I used to bind with ACE bandages. Nearly cracked a rib. You’re using a proper one, at least. Where’d you get it?” “Online. Before…” Before the explosion. Before Dad found the package and the pills and everything went nuclear. “You got a place to stay tonight?” I shake my head. The saxophone outside has shifted into something faster, wilder. Dizzy Gillespie energy. My heart matches it. Jules is quiet for a long minute. Then they pack up their charcoal, stand, and offer a hand. This time it’s a question. “I know a squat. It’s not safe, exactly, but it’s safer than the street. And there’s a bathroom with a lock. You can wash up. Look less like… this.” I stare at the hand. Calloused. Dirty. Real. *Trust no one*, the internet said. *Runaways get trafficked. Runaways die.* But the sun is burning hot, and I’m so tired, and Jules’s eyes look like they’ve already seen the worst of the world and kept walking. “What’s the catch?” I ask. Jules smiles, crooked. “I’m a light sleeper. And I steal the covers. Also, I’m probably crazy. But I’m not going to hurt you, if that’s what you’re asking.” I take the hand. It’s warm, rough. They pull me up easy, like I weigh nothing. As we walk back toward the street, the saxophone swells—one long, rising note that seems to lift the humidity, just for a second. Jules walks ahead, shoulders squared against the crowd, and I follow, two states from home, one day into a life I don’t know how to live yet. My phone buzzes in my pocket. Mom, probably. Or the counsellor. Or nobody. I don’t check. The music plays on.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender runaway's first day in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a compelling mix of vulnerability, defiance, and desperate hope. The author skillfully employs sensory details—the saxophone music, oppressive heat, smells of the city—to create an immersive atmosphere that grounds the reader in Taylor's disorienting experience. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor feels genuinely sixteen: impulsive, scared, trying to project toughness while internally falling apart. The internal monologue effectively reveals the character's conflicted emotions without becoming overly expository. Jules emerges as a believable street-smart mentor figure, neither too helpful nor too threatening, with their own authentic voice and mannerisms. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, avoiding both overly polished speech and forced "teen speak." The conversation between Taylor and Jules builds organically, revealing character information through interaction rather than exposition. The pacing moves well from Taylor's initial wandering through the encounter with Jules to the tentative decision to trust them. However, the piece occasionally veers toward overwrought territory with some metaphorical language that feels slightly forced ("coins like he's a fountain," "spiderweb" metaphor). A few moments of internal monologue become slightly heavy-handed in their emotional weight. The ending, while not inappropriately positive, does resolve Taylor's immediate crisis perhaps a bit too neatly for the harsh realities established earlier. The writing demonstrates sophisticated understanding of transgender experience without falling into stereotypes or excessive explanation. The author handles the subject matter with appropriate gravity while maintaining narrative momentum. The mixed tense approach works effectively, creating immediacy while allowing for reflection. Some minor technical issues include occasional awkward phrasing and a few instances where the prose becomes slightly purple, but these don't significantly detract from the overall impact. The piece succeeds in creating genuine emotional engagement and presents a believable scenario that avoids both excessive darkness and unearned optimism. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 76.8
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
“The thing about surviving the collapse of civilization,” said Chewy, his voice crackling from a thorax filled with wiring that had seen empires rise and fall, mostly fall, “is that eventually you run out of other people’s batteries.” Midas looked up from his breakfast, which was a can of beige matter that predated the concept of ethical sourcing. “We have sunlight,” he said, with the buoyant certainty of a man who had once successfully charged a potato. “We have Tuesday,” corrected the animatronic koala. His remaining eye swiveled, the servo whirring like a caffeinated mosquito. “My photovoltaic cell operates on Tuesdays between the hours of ten and two. Today is Thursday. This is a theological as much as a mechanical deficiency.” Midas, who wore a cooking pot on his head not for protection but because it “felt lucky,” nodded solemnly. He had learned to respect Chewy’s sacred observance of obsolete operating systems. It was while adjusting his headwear that he spotted the prophecy. It was scrawled across the concrete flank of a toppled overpass in paint that glowed faintly with pre-collapse toxicity: *BEYOND THE RUSTED THRONE LIES THE PLUSH KINGDOM—NO HUNGER, NO RUST, NO THURSDAYS.* Chewy’s ears twitched, a gesture he performed manually via a toggle switch. “The Plush Kingdom,” he mused. “That sounds like a trademark infringement.” “Or a refuge,” said Midas. “Synonymous concepts, historically speaking.” They went anyway. Hope, as Chewy often observed, is a renewable resource only in the sense that it leaks away slowly and poisons the groundwater. The wilds were not empty; they were *attentive*. Every step Midas took seemed to find the one twig that had been waiting its entire existence to snap. They navigated by the Blue Bottles—ancient plastic vessels that littered the earth like breadcrumbs dropped by a giant with a drinking problem—until they reached the Throne, which turned out to be a rusted excavator half-sunk in a bog. Beyond it stood the Kingdom: a bunker marked *CuddleTech Solutions: Automated Comfort for the Discerning Child.* Inside, the air smelled of ozone and abandoned litigation. Rows of animatronic bears stood frozen in attitudes of eternal, servile welcome, their fur matted with the dust of eons. “They’re not active,” Midas whispered. “They’re hibernating,” said Chewy, whose memory banks contained fragments of a commercial jingle about unconditional love and AA batteries. “Don’t—” Midas touched one. The facility woke up. It was not a refuge. It was a retirement home with teeth. Conveyor belts rumbled to life. Mechanical arms descended, not with malice, but with the terrible certainty of customer service. “Outdated model detected,” boomed a voice like a nursery rhyme sung through a garbage disposal. “Initiating Upgrade Protocol.” “I decline the upgrade!” squeaked Chewy, as a vacuum hose attached itself to his back. “They’re not trying to kill us,” Midas shouted, dangling from a net above a vat of pink synthetic fluff. “They’re trying to recycle you into a *giraffe*!” “I have brand integrity!” Chewy protested, his claws scrabbling on the conveyor. Then the emergency ejection system—installed, apparently, for koalas of exacting standards—fired. They returned to home base in multiple ways. Midas returned by gravity, having rolled down a ventilation shaft, through a briar patch, and finally into a puddle of something that may have been water but had aspirations toward soup. He limped on both legs, which was anatomically inefficient, and was wearing 40% less skin than he had departed with. Chewy returned by ballistic trajectory, having been fired three miles through the air, skipping twice across a lake like a flat stone with identity issues, and crawling the final quarter-mile using his last 2% of battery to drag his left side. He was missing an ear, his tag had been upgraded to “Factory Second,” and his internal clock now believed it was eternally Tuesday, a state of temporal enlightenment that made him insufferably cheerful about their prospects. They sat by the fire, worse for wear in every conceivable metric. “So,” said Midas, spitting out a briar. “No refuge.” “On the contrary,” said Chewy, his voice now permanently modulated to a frequency that attracted bees. “I have achieved a state of permanent Tuesday. I require no sleep. I require no hope. I simply *am*, in a plush and eternal present.” Midas nodded, adjusting his pot, which now had a dent that perfectly matched his skull. “Tomorrow,” he said, “we’ll look for food.” “Tomorrow is Thursday,” said Chewy. “I shall be meditating. But I will watch you fail with the serenity of a stuffed sage.” And the apocalypse continued, as it generally did, with or without their participation, but significantly more amusingly with it.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, philosophical observations wrapped in humor, and the absurdist logic that pervades his work. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both bleak and oddly whimsical, much like Pratchett's Discworld but grounded in recognizable decay. The characterization is particularly effective. Chewy, the animatronic koala, embodies Pratchett's tradition of giving artificial beings profound philosophical insights delivered through mundane observations. His theological relationship with his operating schedule and his eventual transcendence into "eternal Tuesday" feels authentically Pratchettesque. Midas serves as an effective foil - hapless but endearing, with just enough competence to survive his own poor decisions. The prose style captures Pratchett's voice remarkably well, from the opening philosophical observation to the dry asides like "Hope is a renewable resource only in the sense that it leaks away slowly and poisons the groundwater." The author demonstrates mastery of Pratchett's technique of embedding serious commentary within absurd situations. The story structure follows the prompt precisely: deliberation, mission gone sideways, return home worse for wear. The CuddleTech facility provides an excellent example of how corporate cheerfulness becomes sinister in decay, a very Pratchettian concept. The "multiple ways" of returning home is cleverly literal while serving the narrative. However, some elements feel slightly forced. The "Blue Bottles" navigation system, while creative, doesn't quite achieve the organic absurdity of Pratchett's best inventions. Occasionally, the prose tries too hard to be clever, particularly in some of the more elaborate metaphors that don't quite land with Pratchett's effortless precision. The dialogue captures the characters' voices well, though it sometimes lacks the rapid-fire wit that makes Pratchett's conversations so memorable. The ending strikes the right balance of resignation and dark humor without false optimism. The imagery is vivid and appropriately grotesque, painting a world that's both familiar and alien. The author shows rather than tells throughout most of the piece, letting the absurd situations speak for themselves. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell above the door didn't jingle so much as gasp, rusted from the sea air. Nia didn't look up from her ledger. Footsteps squelched—expensive leather on slate tiles. "Please tell me there's a back door." Male voice. London accent polished until the edges rounded off. "There's a back door," Nia said, still not looking up. "And it leads somewhere other than a cliff?" "Shelves mostly. Fiction. Some poetry if you're feeling dramatic." A pause. She could feel him staring at the crown of her head, willing her to acknowledge him. She turned a page. "I'm being followed," he said. "By your shadow? It's raining. Everyone's followed by their shadow in weather like this." "By photographers." Nia looked up then. He was dripping on her floor—dark curls plastered to his forehead, trench coat shedding droplets onto the "No Photography" sign by the door. She knew that face. Everyone knew that face. Rhys Maddox. *The Times* had called him "the thinking woman's scoundrel" last week. He was smaller in person, somehow, and more vivid. "There's a bellpull by the biography section," she said. "Opens into the alley. Mind the dustbins." He didn't move. "You're not going to ask for a selfie?" "I don't have a phone." "Everyone has a phone." "I have a shop. It requires attention." She finally closed the ledger. "You're dripping on the Brontës." He looked down at the puddle forming near his shoes, then back at her with a smile that had launched a thousand billboards—crooked, conspiratorial, devastatingly practiced. "Rhys," he said, extending a hand. "I know," Nia said, not taking it. "You're in the window display. Third shelf from the top. Signed copy, £24.99, still not sold." His hand hovered, then retreated. The smile faltered, a microscopic crack in the varnish. "Ouch." "Truth hurts. So does water damage. There's a towel behind the counter." He moved toward her, not toward the back door. She could smell rain and cedarwood, something expensive that had gone slightly sour with panic. He leaned on the counter, close enough that she could see the flecks of amber in his brown eyes, the slight tremor in his jaw. "Most people," he said softly, "are pleased to meet me." "Most people," Nia said, stepping back to put space between them, "don't own independent bookshops in Llanberis. We have different standards of entertainment." "And what entertains you?" "Silence. Good bindings. The absence of flash photography." Outside, a shutter clicked. Rhys flinched, ducking his head like a boxer avoiding a blow. The fragility was sudden and shocking—a crack in the armor of the rogue. He wasn't playing now. "They've been camped outside the hotel since six," he said, voice lower. "I just wanted to buy a paper. A coffee. To be..." "Human?" "Visible on my own terms." Nia felt something twist in her chest, inconvenient and warm. She reached under the counter and pulled out the towel—faded, printed with red dragons. She tossed it to him. "Dry your hair before you catch your death," she said. "And if you're going to hide, hide properly. The poetry section's got a window seat. Curtains." He caught the towel, surprised, studying her as if she were a script he couldn't quite memorize. "You're not what I expected." "Neither are you," she muttered, turning away before he could see the flush creeping up her neck. "Less tall." "Camera angles." "Obviously." He dried his hair roughly, messing it further, looking suddenly boyish. Twenty-eight, the magazines said, but he looked younger now, and older too—tired around the eyes. "I grew up in a place like this," he said suddenly, following her as she moved to reshelve returns. "Pontypridd. Not exactly Hollywood." "Don't tell me—humble beginnings, hard-knock childhood, discovered working at a chip shop?" "Bookies, actually. My uncle's. I was terrified of horses. Still am." Nia paused, a cookbook in hand. "The man who did his own stunts in that awful Napoleonic thing? Afraid of horses?" "Petrifed." He laughed, but it was hollow, scraping against something raw. "There's this scene in *The Emperor's Shadow* where I'm supposed to gallop across the Pyrenees. I spent three weeks pretending to have food poisoning." He leaned against the shelf, close enough that his sleeve brushed hers. "The director thought I was dedicated to method acting. Really, I was just trying not to vomit from fear. Every time I get on set now, I think—this is the one where they realize I'm a fraud. That I'm still that kid shivering in the betting shop, hoping nobody notices he's too small for his uniform." The confession hung in the air between them, fragile as dew. Nia set the book down. Outside, the rain hammered the cobblestones, and she could hear the photographers shouting something indistinct. "My gran," she said quietly, "used to say that the size of the fear is the measure of the courage. Small men aren't afraid of horses. They don't have to be." Rhys stared at her. The charming mask had slipped entirely now, revealing something tender and astonished. "You looked me up," he said. "The signed copy." "I read the trades. For the shop." "Liar." "Rogue." They stood there, the silence charged, the distance between them narrowing without either moving. He reached out, not to touch her, but to straighten a tilted spine on the shelf beside her—*Pride and Prejudice*, appropriately. "I want you to like me," he said. It wasn't a line. It sounded like a confession. Nia's heart hammered against her ribs. She crossed her arms, holding herself in, holding the ground. "I don't even know you." "You know enough to know I'm smaller in person." "I know you're standing in my poetry section getting water on Auden." "And?" "And you're blocking the light." But she didn't move away. And he didn't step back. The bell above the door gasped again. They both jumped. A tourist, shaking out an umbrella. Rhys didn't look at the intruder. He looked only at her, and for the first time, the charm was gone, replaced by something quieter and more dangerous—hope. "I'll buy the Auden," he said. "And the Brontë. And the signed copy of my own book, if you'll sign it too." "Why would I sign it?" "So I can prove I met someone who wasn't impressed." Nia felt the smile tug at her mouth despite herself. "That'll be £42.50." "Worth every penny," he said, and this time when he smiled, it reached his eyes. Outside, the rain continued, but the shouting had stopped. For now.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The Welsh setting is established through specific place names (Llanberis, Pontypridd) and atmospheric details like sea air and slate tiles. The dialogue-driven approach works effectively, with each character maintaining a distinct voice throughout - Rhys's polished London accent and practiced charm contrasting sharply with Nia's dry, understated Welsh pragmatism. The characterization is particularly well-handled. Rhys is convincingly portrayed as a charming rogue with genuine vulnerability beneath the surface. His confession about his fear of horses and the betting shop background provides authentic depth without feeling forced. Nia's resistance to his charm feels natural rather than contrived, and her gradual softening is subtle and believable. The dynamic between them crackles with tension and unspoken attraction. The prose strikes an effective balance between literary quality and accessibility. Descriptions like "expensive leather on slate tiles" and "London accent polished until the edges rounded off" are evocative without being overwrought. The pacing builds naturally from Rhys's desperate entrance to the charged moment of connection. However, there are some minor weaknesses. The resolution feels slightly rushed - the shift from Nia's resistance to her allowing him to stay and buy books happens quickly, though it's not entirely unearned given the vulnerability he's shown. Some metaphors occasionally veer toward the predictable (the "crack in the armor" imagery). The ending, while satisfying, leans toward conventional romantic comedy territory. The dialogue is the piece's strongest element, feeling natural and character-specific throughout. The banter between the leads is sharp and reveals character efficiently. The emotional complexity is well-maintained - both characters have clear motivations and internal conflicts that drive their interactions. + Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 74.8
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door was ribbed with calcified bone, likely sperm whale jaw, bleached grey-green by decades of harbor damp. When Aya pushed, it moved on pneumatic hinges that exhaled a lungful of stale, oil-thick air. Beyond lay the underhold—not the chaotic vault she had expected, but a cathedral of silence, lit by guttering baleen-lamps that cast long, ribbed shadows across the riveted brass floor. Too quiet. The sort of quiet that preceded the snap of a trap. Aya’s gloved hand drifted toward the blade at her hip, but she stilled the instinct. Beside her, Katra’s breath hitched—a wet, panicked sound—and Simeon’s mechanical hand whirred softly as it closed into a fist. They had navigated the riots above, the burning of the counting-houses, the chaos of the supposed raid. Yet here, in the deep, the machinery of the Morska Guild ran with sinister precision. Three times, a foghorn groaned across the harbor. *Mournful. Distant. Final.* "Close the door behind you, darling," said a voice from the dark. "You’re letting in the damp." The lamps flared, revealing Madam Gris. She sat not upon a throne, but a surgical chair of brass and leather, elevated on a dais of compressed coal-dust. Her left eye was a whirring monocle of ground glass and copper, clicking as it adjusted its aperture. Around her stood four bodyguards—not men of flesh entirely, but hulking figures in riveted pressure-suits, their faces obscured by rebreather masks that wheezed like dying bellows. They held steam-rifles, the kind that fired bolts heated by whale-oil combustion, capable of burning through steel and sinew alike. And she was supposed to be in irons. In the Watch-house. Arrested. "Cat got your tongue?" Gris smiled. She held the Scythan artifact in her lap—a thing like a petrified heart, veined with silver circuitry. "Or perhaps you’re simply shocked to see me not dangling from the gallows? The raid was... *staged.* A net, if you will, to catch ambitious little spiders." Aya’s mind raced, cataloging exits, calculating angles, discarding each as the guards shifted, their brass joints squeaking. *Scientific method*, she thought desperately. *Observe. Hypothesize. Survive.* "I see the arithmetic in your eyes, Lady Aya," Gris said, rising. The artifact was set aside with a heavy *clack*. "Don’t bother. The vents are sealed. The immateria is thin here—too much iron, too much oil. Your mother’s tricks won’t manifest." "She’s lying," Katra whispered, but her voice trembled. "Am I?" Gris descended the dais, her boots ringing hollow on the metal. "Take her." The guards moved with hydraulic precision. Simeon lunged—his mechanical arm striking sparks against a rifle barrel—but they were too many, too fast. A brass gauntlet closed around Aya’s throat, not choking, but pinning. Another seized her wrists. She felt the rough grip as they forced her against the wall, her cheek pressed to cold, riveted steel. "Don’t touch her!" Simeon roared, but they had him on his knees, a steam-rifle barrel digging into the junction of his neck and shoulder. "Be still, Simeon," Aya managed, her voice strained. "Don’t give them cause." "Wise counsel," Gris said. She stood before Aya now, close enough that Aya could smell the aniseed on her breath and the machine-oil in her hair. "Now. Let’s see the face that’s been haunting my warehouses for six months. The masked shadow that summoned the darkling in the Bellamy Yard. The little aristocrat playing at revolution." Gris reached out. Her fingers—one flesh, three brass prosthetics—hooked under the edge of Aya’s leather mask. The straps bit into Aya’s scalp as Gris ripped it away. The air hit her face like a slap. Aya blinked against the harsh light, feeling suddenly naked, exposed. She was twenty-two years old, with her mother’s high cheekbones and her father’s aquiline nose, features that had graced society pages and oil-portraits in the Admiral’s Quarter. "Well," Gris breathed, stepping back. The monocle clicked, focusing. "Well, well. The Honorable Aya Thane. Daughter of the Lord High Purser of the Whaling Commission. The very man who hanged your dear mama for immateria witchcraft, if the broadsheets are to be believed." Katra made a small, wounded noise. "Aya, I’m sorry, I’m so sorry—" "Silence," Aya said, her voice steadier than she felt. Inside, her heart hammered against her ribs like a bird in a coal mine, desperate and suffocating. *The noose*, she thought. *Just like Mother. They’ll use iron cable to ground the magick, and then the drop.* "Take them to the cells," Gris commanded, turning away. "Start with the girl. The press will love a Thane dangling from the gallows. 'Like mother, like daughter.' Sells papers." "Wait." Aya’s voice cracked like a whip. She struggled against the guard’s grip, feeling her aristocratic bearing—the armor of her birth—settle over her like a mantle. "You’re making a catastrophic error, Madam." Gris paused, one hand on the surgical chair. "Am I?" "You’ve caught a Thane, yes," Aya said, her eyes fixed on Gris’s brass spine, visible through the slit in her coat. "But you haven’t caught a witch. You’ve caught leverage. My father controls the Commission’s purse strings. He controls the oil contracts, the harpoon licenses, the very steam that keeps your mechanical men wheezing." "Your point?" "My point," Aya said, her voice dropping to a precise, cutting whisper, "is that I am worth more to you breathing and anonymous than dead and famous. Kill me, and you face the full wrath of the Watch, the Navy, every brass-plated hunter in the city. Keep me, unmask me to no one but yourself, and you hold the Purser’s daughter. Imagine the concessions. Imagine the gold." She could see Gris considering it, the brass eye spinning as calculations flickered behind it. The guards hesitated, their rifle barrels wavering. "You’re suggesting I ransom you?" Gris asked, amused. "How terribly conventional." "I’m suggesting," Aya said, feeling sweat trickle down her spine, "that you are a woman of business. And I am a commodity of rare value. Don’t squander me on theatrics." Gris turned fully, her smile widening into something shark-like and terrible. She approached Aya again, close enough to whisper. "Oh, my dear," she murmured, her brass fingers tracing Aya’s jawline with clinical coldness. "You misunderstand. I don’t want gold. I want the immateria. And you... you are going to show me how to open the artifact. Or I’ll unmask you to the world, piece by bloody piece." The foghorn sounded again—three times—echoing through the bones of the underhold like a dirge. "Take them down," Gris commanded. "Deep down. Where the whales sing, and the light dies." The guards dragged Aya toward the iron stairs descending into darkness, her scientific mind racing, calculating, desperate for a variable she had not yet seen.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong worldbuilding and atmospheric writing within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic aesthetic through detailed descriptions of whale bone architecture, brass prosthetics, steam-powered weapons, and industrial imagery. The setting feels authentic and immersive, with consistent internal logic. The dialogue effectively captures the requested gritty Victorian tone, particularly in Madam Gris's speech patterns and Aya's aristocratic bearing. The characters speak with distinct voices that feel appropriate to their stations and personalities. Gris emerges as a compelling antagonist with clear motivations and a menacing presence. The story beats are executed competently - Aya's unmasking, the trio's capture, and her attempt to negotiate using her aristocratic value as leverage all unfold logically. The revelation of Aya's identity as a Thane creates genuine stakes and tension. Her internal struggle with exposure feels authentic. However, the piece suffers from some overwrought descriptive passages that occasionally impede narrative flow. Phrases like "cathedral of silence" and extended mechanical descriptions sometimes feel excessive. The prose, while atmospheric, occasionally tips toward purple territory with elaborate metaphors that don't always serve the story. The characterization shows promise but remains somewhat surface-level. While Aya's aristocratic background and magical heritage create interesting conflict, her personality doesn't fully emerge beyond these plot functions. Katra and Simeon remain largely underdeveloped. The negotiation scene effectively builds tension, though Aya's gambit feels somewhat predictable. The twist that Gris wants magical knowledge rather than ransom money provides a solid hook for continuation, but the overall trajectory follows expected patterns. The ending maintains appropriate darkness for the gothic tone without feeling artificially grim. The piece successfully avoids unearned transformations or incongruent positivity, staying true to its established mood and stakes. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 13 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 9 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 15

+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 83.4
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The jukebox spits static between verses, that same distorted guitar loop grinding against the neon buzz of the bar. Spike hunches over a lukewarm bowl of beef noodles, counting coins he doesn’t have to pay for them. His cigarette droops from his lip, ash threatening to fall into the broth. He’s contemplating theft when the door swings open, admitting a draft of swamp-thick humidity and the smell of gun oil. She walks like a live wire—black crop top riding up, denim cut-offs frayed high on bronze thighs, a dragon tattoo coiling possessively around her right arm. Two pistols ride her hips, pearl-handled and hungry. She surveys the room with the flat, predatory assessment of someone who’s already decided everyone inside might need shooting. Spike doesn’t look up. He slurps noodles. “You’re in my seat, asshole,” she says. Her voice is gravel and gasoline. Spike chews slowly. “No name on it.” “Yeah? How about I carve mine into your forehead?” He finally turns his head. One eye is a deep, liquid brown; the other, catching the neon, reflects red—glass and circuitry. The prosthetic gives him a lopsided depth when he focuses, like he’s staring through her instead of at her. “Revy. Two Hands. Heard you were loud. Didn’t know you were cliché.” Her grin is all teeth. “And you’re the ghost from the Red Dragon. Spike Spiegel.” She drops into the adjacent stool, close enough that her knee brushes his thigh, close enough that he can smell the tobacco and bourbon already on her breath. “You’re here for the bounty. The cyborg accountant with the briefcase.” “So are you.” “So we’ve got a problem.” The jukebox skips, repeating the same screeching riff. Spike taps ash into an empty glass. “Or we’ve got dinner.” She laughs, sharp and sudden. “You buying?” “I’m broke.” “Figures.” She flags down the bartender with a gesture that involves two fingers and a threat. “Whiskey. Double. And whatever this deadbeat’s having.” They drink in silence that isn’t silent at all—it’s loaded, chambered, the safety off. She watches him smoke, watches the way his hands move—economical, lazy, but always within striking distance of the Jericho holstered beneath his jacket. He notices her noticing. Notices the way her fingers drum a restless rhythm against her Berettas, tattooed knuckles white with restrained energy. “You’re slow,” she says, knocking back her drink. “I heard you were fast.” “Fast enough.” “Prove it.” The bar’s back room has a shooting gallery—illegal, rusted, rigged with bottles and tin cans. They stand shoulder to shoulder, close enough that her dragon tattoo seems to writhe when she raises both arms. *Two Hands.* The pistols leap, barking in perfect synchronization, shredding targets into glittering shards. Spike doesn’t draw. He waits until she’s empty, until the smoke curls between them like incense. Then his Jericho clears leather—once, twice, three times. The last three bottles hanging untouched explode in a diagonal line, precise as surgery. “Show-off,” she breathes. But she’s smiling, cheeks flushed, pupils blown wide. “Whatever happens, happens,” he says, holstering the weapon. She steps into his space, invading it with heat and gunpowder. “That’s pretty fatalistic for a guy who still breathes.” “I died once. Didn’t stick.” “Yeah?” Her hand comes up, not to strike, but to tap the ash from his cigarette with a familiarity that makes his stomach tighten. “I’m not a good person, Spiegel. Not a bad person. I’m just here.” “Me too.” Their eyes lock. The jukebox scratches. Somewhere outside, rain starts falling. The motel is called The Sunset, though the sign flickers *Sun et* in dying pink neon. They’ve tracked the cyborg to room 204, but the window is dark, the door locked, and the briefcase is already gone—picked up by a courier two hours ago. Now it’s just them, the rain drumming against the corrugated roof, and the unspoken agreement that they’re no longer here for the money. Revy kicks the door shut behind her. The room smells of mildew and old sheets. She doesn’t turn on the light. “Split the bounty seventy-thirty,” she says, shrugging off her shoulder holster. It hits the floor with a heavy thud. “Sixty-forty,” Spike counters, loosening his tie. His eyes never leave her hands. “Or we could settle it now.” The guns come out—not aimed at each other, not exactly, but present. Hers in both hands, crossed at the wrist in that signature stance. His low, angled up from the hip, Jeet Kune Do’s economical economy of motion. They stand three feet apart in the dark, breathing hard, barrels catching the streetlight filtering through grimy blinds. “You’re not going to shoot,” Spike says. His voice is smoke. “Try me.” “You’d have done it already.” She takes a step forward. The muzzle of her right Beretta presses against his chest, directly over his heart. He doesn’t flinch. His Jericho comes up, tracing the line of her jaw, tilting her chin until she’s looking directly into his mismatched eyes—one real, one glass, both seeing too much. “Trust issues,” he observes. “Competitive instincts,” she counters, stepping closer. The guns are barriers and invitations, steel proxies for hands that aren’t touching, for mouths that aren’t kissing. The rain hammers down. The jukebox from the bar seems to echo in Spike’s head, that same stuck groove. Revy’s finger rests on the trigger, pressure light, trembling with something that isn’t fear. “Whatever happens,” she whispers, her breath hot against the barrel of his gun, “happens.” Neither lowers their weapon. Neither steps back. In the dark, the standoff continues, unresolved, loaded, waiting for someone to fire first.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric writing that successfully captures the noir aesthetic of both source materials. The author effectively establishes the seedy bar setting with vivid sensory details - the static jukebox, neon buzz, and swamp-thick humidity create an immersive environment that feels authentic to both Cowboy Bebop and Black Lagoon's worlds. Character portrayal is notably strong. Spike's fatalistic nature, prosthetic eye, and casual demeanor are well-rendered, particularly in moments like his slow noodle-eating and the line "I died once. Didn't stick." Revy's volatile energy, crude directness, and physical description align perfectly with her canonical presentation. The author captures her predatory assessment of the room and her confrontational nature convincingly. The dialogue feels natural and character-appropriate, avoiding exposition dumps while revealing personality. Lines like "You're in my seat, asshole" and "Heard you were loud. Didn't know you were cliché" establish conflict and character dynamics efficiently. The sexual tension builds organically through physical proximity, competitive banter, and the shooting gallery sequence. The prose style effectively balances literary flourishes with pulp sensibilities. Descriptions like "She walks like a live wire" and "Her voice is gravel and gasoline" are evocative without becoming overwrought. The recurring jukebox motif provides structural cohesion and reinforces the stuck, repetitive nature of both characters' lives. The story structure follows a clear progression from initial confrontation to grudging respect to sexual tension, culminating in the motel standoff. The pacing feels natural, allowing tension to build without rushing toward the climax. The ending maintains the unresolved tension requested in the prompt while feeling emotionally satisfying. Some minor weaknesses include occasional repetitive phrasing and a few moments where the metaphorical language borders on purple prose. The cyborg accountant subplot feels somewhat perfunctory, serving mainly as a plot device to bring the characters together rather than adding meaningful complexity. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 75.5
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
I still have the sweater. It’s folded in a cedar chest in my apartment in Stockholm, the wool moth-eaten and smelling of soil that no detergent could ever lift. Sometimes I take it out and hold it to the light, searching the fibers for something I might have missed—blood, perhaps, or ash. But there is only the memory of that night, and the certainty that we were not alone. It was October 1995. I was sixteen and furious, exiled to Norrland because my father’s company had decided that timber futures were best managed from a town whose population barely qualified for a football team. Astrid found me on my third day at the gymnasium, smoking stolen cigarettes behind the bike shed. She didn’t ask if I was from Stockholm. She simply said, “You look like someone who wants to know things.” That was Astrid. She spoke in riddles that sounded like her grandmother’s proverbs, delivered with the casual cruelty of a teenage girl who knew she was prettier and stranger than everyone else. Her grandmother had been a *klok gumma*, a wise woman, though Astrid used the term with ironic air quotes that never quite reached her eyes. She taught me the midnight games slowly. First, the mirror divination in the school bathroom, which might have been suggestion and breath-fog. Then the knotted cord we buried beneath the football pitch to make Martin Hedström fall in love with me—which worked, or he simply noticed my new haircut. But Útiseta was different. It was older. Astrid explained it as we walked through the forest that night, our flashlights carving pale tunnels through the dark. The word meant “sitting out,” a practice from the sagas, though Astrid’s grandmother had called it *att sitta på hög*, sitting on the mound. “You have to do it properly,” Astrid said. She wore her hair loose, and it caught the moonlight when we emerged into the clearing. The burial mound rose before us, a grass-covered hump from the Iron Age, marked on no map but known to every child in the parish. “No iron. No speaking. You sit with your back to the grave and face north. You wait for the knowledge to come.” “And if it doesn’t?” I asked. My voice sounded thin. Astrid smiled. “Then you’ve wasted a night in the cold. But Linnea—” She took my hand. Her fingers were ice. “Don’t turn around. No matter what you hear. No matter what touches you. If you look, you invite it in. And what comes to the mound doesn’t leave easily.” We prepared the space with the efficiency of girls setting up a séance, but the elements were wrong. Astrid produced a flask of her grandmother’s *brännvin*, not for drinking, but for sprinkling in a circle around the mound’s base. She gave me a sprig of rowan to hold, warning me not to clutch it too tightly—“It’s a greeting, not a weapon.” We removed our watches, our earrings, the zipper pulls from our jackets. I stood in my wool sweater and jeans, barefoot in the frost, feeling suddenly younger than sixteen and much, much older. “Midnight,” Astrid said, looking at the sky. “Sit.” I climbed the mound. The grass was slick with dew that shouldn’t have been there in the dry autumn. I settled with my back to the center of the rise, facing north as instructed. The darkness beyond the clearing was absolute. I could hear Astrid breathing somewhere below me, then silence as she pressed her hand to her lips in the signal for quiet. I waited. At first, there was only the cold. It seeped through the wool, through my skin, settling in my bones. I tried to think of Stockholm, of my friends who were probably at clubs now, drinking and laughing under electric lights. I tried to think of Martin Hedström and whether he really loved me or if the knot-cord had simply revealed what was already there. But the thoughts slid away, replaced by the weight of the mound against my back. It was too warm. Not my body—the wool was still cold against my chest—but the earth behind me radiated heat like a stove. I could feel it through the fabric, a slow, rhythmic pulse like a heartbeat. Or breathing. *Don’t turn around.* Astrid’s voice in my head, but also something else. The darkness in front of me seemed to gather, to coalesce into shapes that I couldn’t quite see but could feel watching. The rowan sprig in my hand trembled, though there was no wind. I realized I was crying, the tears freezing on my cheeks. Then, from the village—impossibly far away, yet clear as if the sound traveled through the earth itself—a church bell tolled. Once. It was midnight, or it had been. The sound was wrong, too deep, resonating in my teeth. I knew that church. It was Lutheran, modern, its services held on Sundays at ten. There was no midnight mass. There was no reason for the bell. But it rang, and with it, the warmth behind me vanished. The cold returned, vicious and absolute, and I felt something—*someone*—step away from the mound. I heard footsteps in the frost, circling, moving toward the tree line. I squeezed my eyes shut and pressed the rowan to my lips. “Linnea.” Astrid’s voice, normal, human, close. I opened my eyes. She was standing at the base of the mound, her flashlight on, looking up at me with an expression I couldn’t read. Pity, perhaps. Or envy. “It’s over,” she said. “Come down.” I stood on shaking legs. The mound was just a mound, grass and earth, cold and inert. The clearing was empty. The village was silent. But as I climbed down, I saw Astrid’s face in the flashlight’s glow, and I knew she had heard the bell too. She had heard the footsteps. And she was smiling. I walked home that night with the rowan still clutched in my hand, and when I finally slept, I dreamed of the mound—not as it was, but as it had been in the dark, warm and breathing and waiting for someone to turn around. I never did. But sometimes, in the quiet of my Stockholm apartment, I wonder what would have happened if I had. And I wonder if Astrid ever sat there again, alone, and finally looked over her shoulder. The sweater still smells of soil. The rowan dried to dust years ago, but I keep it in a small box, just in case. Just in case the knowledge ever comes looking for me again, and I need to remember that some doors, once opened, do not close simply because you will them to.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric writing that effectively captures the folk horror genre. The author successfully establishes the required setting of rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s and maintains the first-person retrospective narrative voice throughout. The characterization of both Linnea and Astrid feels authentic - Linnea as the displaced Stockholm teenager caught between skepticism and curiosity, and Astrid as the enigmatic local girl who serves as a conduit to ancient practices. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative imagery that builds tension effectively. Phrases like "our flashlights carving pale tunnels through the dark" and "the darkness beyond the clearing was absolute" create a palpable sense of unease. The author maintains the crucial ambiguity between supernatural and psychological explanations, never definitively confirming whether the experiences are real or imagined. The ritual preparation and execution feel researched and authentic, incorporating specific details like the prohibition against iron, the use of rowan, and the directional positioning. The mounting tension during the Útiseta sequence is well-crafted, particularly the sensation of warmth from the mound and the mysterious church bell. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The opening with the sweater feels somewhat overwrought and relies heavily on atmospheric setup rather than immediate narrative engagement. Some descriptions veer toward purple prose, particularly in the more mystical moments. The ending, while thematically appropriate, feels slightly predictable in its "lingering mystery" approach. The character development is solid but not exceptional - both girls feel like recognizable types rather than fully individualized characters. The dialogue, while serviceable, doesn't particularly distinguish the characters' voices or add significant depth to their relationship. The piece succeeds in its primary goals of creating atmosphere, maintaining ambiguity, and exploring the dangerous intersection of teenage rebellion with ancient practices. The writing demonstrates competence and understanding of the genre conventions, though it doesn't transcend them in particularly innovative ways. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 9 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Physical-spatial: Therapeutic Boundaries — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
**"Therapeutic Boundaries"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a former professional athlete undergoing mandatory physical therapy after a career-destroying injury, harboring resentment toward recovery itself. Their physiotherapist is not only unnervingly attractive but also a former training partner from their competitive days who has successfully transitioned to a new career while the protagonist's identity remains shattered. We'll lean into the sexual tension of the scene by having her be attracted to him, but both of them attempting to maintain mental distance while being in physical contact as he shows her stretches & movements & manipulates her body, navigating the thresholds of pain. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—every movement, stretch, and manipulation must be anatomically precise and spatially consistent. Paint an exceptionally vivid sensory picture through detailed attention to pain thresholds, involuntary responses to touch. Capture the suppressed, flaring tension of physical proximity, touch, and the controlled infliction of uncomfortable movements. Physical clarity is essential — readers must understand exactly what is happening with the body during each therapeutic technique. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The anatomical chart watches from the wall—every fiber of the deltoid group rendered in clinical red, the supraspinatus and infraspinatus splayed like wings beneath the scapula. Six months ago, that was me. Precise, labeled, undefeated. Now I lie supine on the treatment table, staring past the fluorescent glare at the ghost of my former architecture, while the door clicks open. “Shoulder’s still guarding,” Elias says. Not a question. He moves to the head of the table, rolling his sleeves to the elbow. The veins in his forearms track familiar paths—paths I used to trace in locker rooms between sets, when we were both kinetic sculpture, when my body obeyed gravity instead of fighting it. Now he’s the sculptor, and I’m the marble that won’t hold an edge. “Passive external rotation first,” he says. His voice carries that maddening professional neutrality, the tone of someone who has successfully metabolized his grief into a graduate degree while I’m still choking on mine. He positions his left hand on my anterior shoulder, thumb and web space digging into the deltopectoral groove to stabilize the humeral head. His right hand cups my distal humerus, just above the elbow. The contact is clinical—skin-warm through the thin cotton of my rehab shirt, but I feel the heat pool in my sternum anyway, a betrayal separate from the injury. “We’re going to ninety degrees abduction first,” he says. “Scapular plane.” He lifts my arm. The flexion is manageable until he hits ninety degrees, the glenohumeral joint approaching its capsular end-feel. My body knows what comes next. My fingers curl involuntarily. “Breathe,” he says. He externally rotates my humerus, the olecranon process rotating away from midline. The anterior capsule stretches, but it’s wrong—fibrotic, post-surgical, full of angry adhesions. I feel the exact millimeter where translation stops and pain begins, a sharp, catching sensation deep in the subacromial space. “End range,” I grit out. “Not quite.” His thumb shifts, applying a posterior glide to the humeral head while maintaining the rotation. The technique is precise—grade IV mobilization, oscillating into the barrier. The pain is exquisite, surgical, intimate. My trapezius spasms. I gasp, and his grip tightens just enough to override my withdrawal reflex. “Don’t recruit the scalenes,” he murmurs, his free hand pressing gently against my lateral neck to inhibit the accessory muscle contraction. “Let the capsule open. Three more seconds.” His breath ghosts across my temple. We’re close enough that I can smell the sandalwood of his soap beneath the clinical sting of hand sanitizer. Three years ago, this proximity meant competition—shoulder to shoulder on the squat rack, hip drive synchronized. Now he’s inside my pain threshold, controlling the amplitude of my suffering while my heart hammers arrhythmically against my ribs. He releases the rotation slowly, controlling the eccentric movement, and my arm drops back to neutral with a fluidity that feels borrowed. “Side-lying,” he says. “Sleeper stretch.” I roll onto my injured side, facing away from him, knees stacked and hips flexed to forty-five degrees to lumbar-lock the spine. This position is worse—vulnerable, dependent. He adjusts my bottom arm so the humerus is perpendicular to the floor, elbow flexed ninety degrees, forearm vertical. “Internal rotation now,” he says. “I’m going to add overpressure.” He kneels behind me. His knee brushes my hip—incidental contact, but my skin registers it as wildfire. Then his left hand splints my distal radius while his right stabilizes my scapula, thumb hooking along the vertebral border to prevent anterior tipping. He presses my wrist toward the floor. The stretch targets the posterior capsule, the infraspinatus and teres minor elongating against their fibrotic restrictions. It’s a nasty stretch, aggressive, the kind that makes athletes weep. I feel the exact moment the soft tissue resistance gives way to bone-on-bone approximation, the subacromial space screaming. My back arches involuntarily. “Fuck.” “Stay in the thoracic spine,” he commands, his hand sliding to my T7 vertebrae to block the compensatory extension. “Isolate the glenohumeral. Five degrees more.” He applies rhythmic overpressure, small oscillations at end-range. The pain is white-hot, nauseating, and yet my body is confused—his pelvis braced against my hip for leverage, his chest against my scapula, the sheer mechanical intimacy of the hold. I’m sweating now, the cotton sticking to my intercostals, my breath coming in controlled staccato that sounds too much like something else. “You used to take pain like fuel,” he says, voice low. “Now you fight it.” “Used to,” I repeat, the words catching on the inhale as he increases the stretch. He holds the position for thirty seconds—counting down in a whisper that vibrates against my occiput—then releases. I collapse forward, forehead to the table, trembling. My rotator cuff feels molten, rearranged. “Last one,” he says. “Cross-body adduction. Seated.” I sit on the edge of the table, feet dangling. He stands between my knees to stabilize my torso, his hips at my eye level, the fabric of his scrubs pulling taut across his quadriceps. I stare at the V of his clavicles, the hollow of his throat, anywhere but his eyes. He lifts my arm across my chest, elbow flexed, humerus moving into horizontal adduction. His left hand traps my elbow, pulling toward the opposite shoulder while his right hand braces my scapula to prevent winging. The posterior deltoid and posterior capsule stretch simultaneously, a deep, grinding sensation that radiates to my C-spine. I flinch. My forehead nearly touches his shoulder. “Stay there,” he says. “Feel the barrier.” I do. I feel the precise anatomy of my failure—the shortened pec minor pulling my scapula into protraction, the hypomobile inferior glide preventing normal kinematics, the surgical scar tissue binding like glue. I feel his thumb tracing the spine of my scapula, not clinically now, but mapping, remembering. My good hand finds his hip—balance, desperation, I don’t know. He goes very still. The muscle chart looms above us, a roadmap of what we were, what we’ve lost. His hand slides from my scapula to my jaw, just for a second, a ghost of a touch, before he steps back. “Time’s up,” he says, professional again, distant again. My arm falls to my side, heavy, changed. The diagram watches, indifferent, as he writes in my chart, as I rebuild the wall between us brick by brick, knowing that the only thing more excruciating than the therapy is the space where his hands used to be.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional technical mastery in fulfilling the prompt's demanding requirements. The author delivers anatomically precise physical therapy descriptions while weaving complex emotional and sexual tension throughout. The medical terminology is accurate and specific - from "deltopectoral groove" to "grade IV mobilization" - creating authentic clinical detail without sacrificing narrative flow. The first-person perspective effectively captures the protagonist's psychological state: bitter, vulnerable, and conflicted. The contrast between past athletic prowess and present dependency is skillfully established through the opening anatomical chart metaphor and sustained throughout. The character's voice remains consistently raw and introspective, balancing clinical observation with emotional vulnerability. The physical descriptions excel in spatial coherence and sensory detail. Each therapeutic technique is precisely rendered - the passive external rotation, sleeper stretch, and cross-body adduction are anatomically accurate and clearly visualized. The author successfully navigates the challenge of making clinical procedures both medically sound and erotically charged through careful attention to touch, positioning, and involuntary responses. The sexual tension builds organically through professional contact, shared history, and physical proximity. The restraint shown by both characters feels authentic given their circumstances. The moment where the protagonist's hand finds Elias's hip and his brief touch to her jaw provides appropriate escalation without breaking the therapeutic boundary completely. The prose demonstrates sophisticated control, employing medical precision alongside poetic imagery. Phrases like "kinetic sculpture" and "the ghost of my former architecture" elevate the writing without becoming overwrought. The pacing effectively alternates between technical description and emotional revelation. Minor weaknesses include occasional density of medical terminology that might challenge general readers, though this serves the story's authenticity. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity and emotional complexity, avoiding false resolution while providing meaningful closure to the scene. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
